replies.msg:
畢竟人類既現代文明體制係由西方建立，所以西方人大部份時間都把握左世界既話語權。白人把握左對各種事物定義「好」與「壞」既權力，以致於世界所有地方都以白人定義既標準去審視自己。例如日本明治維新，所謂「現代化」就係一個以西方標準否定自身傳統文化既過程。

所以即使走到今日，全世界係文化上仍然充斥各種「西方先進，白人優越」既觀念。依個Po所提到既崇洋現象，以及對「西方」與「白人」種種美好既想像，就係又一例證。

而係西方，即使開始對依個現象有所反思，興起政治正確嘗試推動修正，都被右翼批鬥為「逆向歧視」。事實上，由戴口罩一事，西方人死都唔肯聽亞洲人講，就知道西方中心主義同白人優越思想仍然深入骨髓。

但我要指出，人類現代化過程滲入左西方既主觀意見，不等於現代化本身等同於西方化。中國、俄羅斯一類獨裁國家，經常會借用後殖民話語，將獨裁統治稱為本土傳統文化，指責批評者「西方中心主義」。事實上，「現代性」並唔係單純屬於西方，而係由人類理性既發展所推動，只係發展過程中西方曾經走得比較前。普世價值係屬於普世，而非西方。

replies.msg:
The entire notion of ‘Hongkonger’ as an identity is weak in the international school community. I think identity in general is something that many international school kids (born and raised in HK) struggle with since they are not local enough to mix with locals but simultaneously influenced by ‘Chinese’ values which make them differ from their white peers.

When an individual is unclear about their own identity I suppose they would default to the ‘largest’ group they can identify as. For HK intl school students this is most likely ‘Chinese’ (poorly defined). Associating themselves with a large ethnic group feels safer I suppose, when faced with threats overseas on the grounds of ethnicity.

When you combine the fact that intl school students’ families are usually wealthy and have strong interests linked with the mainland economy it is no wonder that the ‘Chinese’ identity dominates in these households. Further, intl school education usually is lacklustre in Chinese language proficiency which hampers their ability and interest to read local media and take up local information.

quote.msg: 睇李光耀既書你就明 點解坡仔又英又奶共
I mean why they identify as Chinese rather than Hong Konger as a defensive mechanism when discriminated against overseas

The entire notion of ‘Hongkonger’ as an identity is weak in the international school community. I think identity in general is something that many international school kids (born and raised in HK) struggle with since they are not local enough to mix with locals but simultaneously influenced by ‘Chinese’ values which make them differ from their white peers.

When an individual is unclear about their own identity I suppose they would default to the ‘largest’ group they can identify as. For HK intl school students this is most likely ‘Chinese’ (poorly defined). Associating themselves with a large ethnic group feels safer I suppose, when faced with threats overseas on the grounds of ethnicity.

When you combine the fact that intl school students’ families are usually wealthy and have strong interests linked with the mainland economy it is no wonder that the ‘Chinese’ identity dominates in these households. Further, intl school education usually is lacklustre in Chinese language proficiency which hampers their ability and interest to read local media and take up local information.
